slug: 人工语言_世界语为什么失败了但编程语言成功了
title: 人工语言——世界语为什么失败了但编程语言成功了

outline.concept: Network effects create a cold-start trap for spoken languages: a language's value to you depends on how many others speak it. Esperanto faced a chicken-and-egg problem — nobody learns it because nobody speaks it. Programming languages bypass this because even a single user with a compiler gets full utility; the machine is always fluent.
outline.query: 学语言 没人交流 社群 冷启动 network effect 一个人用

outline.concept: Esperanto tried to displace natural languages that already worked for international communication (English, French). Programming languages faced no such incumbent — machines cannot parse human speech, so there was nothing to replace. Filling a vacuum is easier than replacing a working system.
outline.query: 替代 旧工具 已经够用 不需要换 新方法 替换成本

outline.concept: Ambiguity is a feature in human language, not a bug — vagueness, context, and pragmatic inference let speakers be flexible, polite, and creative. Esperanto's rationalized grammar stripped these away, making it worse at being a human language. Programming languages embrace precision because machines need deterministic interpretation; rigidity is the point.
outline.query: 歧义 模糊 语境 言外之意 ambiguity 精确 理解

outline.concept: Communities form around shared practice and tooling, not linguistic elegance. Esperanto offered cultural neutrality but no jobs, frameworks, or problems to solve together. Programming languages accumulate communities through ecosystems — libraries, job markets, conferences — that make the language worth staying in regardless of its design quality.
outline.query: 开发者社区 开源 框架 找工作 生态 ecosystem 工具链
